findings.title: Database credentials stored in plaintext ConfigMap
findings.description: Task 1007 creates a ConfigMap (sigma1-infra-endpoints) that includes password-bearing connection URLs (POSTGRES_URL with embedded password, REDIS_URL with embedded password). ConfigMaps are not encrypted at rest by default in Kubernetes and are visible to anyone with read access to the namespace. The subtask notes this concern but proceeds anyway 'for convenience.'
findings.recommendation: Never embed credentials in ConfigMaps. Store host/port/bucket references in the ConfigMap and reference passwords exclusively via Kubernetes Secrets using secretKeyRef in pod specs. Ensure etcd encryption at rest is enabled for Secrets. Audit all envFrom usages to confirm no secrets leak into ConfigMap values.

findings.title: No authentication middleware defined for any backend API service
findings.description: Tasks 2, 3, 4, 5, and 6 define API endpoints but none include implementation of authentication middleware. Decision point dp-4 identifies the need for auth (JWT or session-based) but no task or subtask is dedicated to implementing a shared auth library, JWT validation middleware, or token issuance service. Rate limiting in task 2006 extracts tenant_id from 'JWT claims, API key header, or X-Tenant-Id header' but there is no task that issues or validates these tokens.
findings.recommendation: Add a dedicated subtask for implementing a shared authentication library/middleware (JWT validation, token issuance, service-to-service auth) before or in parallel with the first API implementation tasks. Each service should integrate this middleware. Define a concrete auth architecture: who issues tokens, what claims they carry, and how they're validated.

findings.title: No RBAC/permissions model for API-level authorization
findings.description: While Kubernetes-level RBAC is addressed in Task 10001, there is no task for application-level authorization (role-based access control within the APIs). Admin endpoints, finance reports, payroll data, and vetting results have no access control differentiation. The Morgan agent skills include an 'admin' skill with 'elevated tool access' (7007) but no authorization check prevents non-admin users from accessing admin-only tools or endpoints.
findings.recommendation: Implement application-level RBAC with defined roles (admin, operator, customer, service-account) and permissions per endpoint. Add authorization middleware that checks JWT claims/roles against required permissions for each route. Document the permission matrix.

findings.title: Stripe webhook signature verification is the only explicit security validation for inbound webhooks
findings.description: Task 4004 correctly implements Stripe webhook signature verification, but no similar verification is described for other inbound webhook/callback scenarios: Twilio webhooks (7003), Signal-CLI message callbacks (7002), and social media API callbacks. These endpoints could be spoofed by external actors to inject malicious data.
findings.recommendation: Implement request signature verification for all inbound webhook endpoints: Twilio request validation (using X-Twilio-Signature), Signal-CLI access restriction (bind to localhost only since it's a sidecar), and any social platform webhook callbacks. Add source IP restrictions or shared secrets where signature verification isn't available.

findings.title: Signal-CLI container uses :latest tag with no image pinning
findings.description: Task 1005 deploys Signal-CLI using 'bbernhard/signal-cli-rest-api:latest' — a third-party image with no version pinning. This creates supply chain risk: the image could be updated with vulnerabilities or malicious code at any time, and builds are not reproducible.
findings.recommendation: Pin the Signal-CLI image to a specific digest or semantic version tag (e.g., bbernhard/signal-cli-rest-api:0.80). Scan the image with Trivy or Grype before deployment. Consider building a custom image from the Signal-CLI source for better control. Apply this image pinning policy to all container images in the project.

findings.title: PII handling not addressed in customer vetting and CRM data flows
findings.description: The Customer Vetting Service (Task 5) collects and stores sensitive PII: company details, credit reports, LinkedIn profiles, and financial data. The Social Media Engine stores photos. The Finance Service stores customer payment information. No tasks address PII classification, data retention policies, encryption of sensitive fields at rest, or data subject access/deletion requests.
findings.recommendation: Add a subtask to classify data sensitivity levels for each service's data model. Implement column-level encryption for highly sensitive fields (credit scores, payment details). Define data retention policies. Plan for data subject access requests (DSAR) and right-to-deletion if operating under GDPR or similar regulations.

findings.title: No container image scanning in CI/CD pipeline
findings.description: No task mentions container image vulnerability scanning. Multiple services build custom Docker images (Rust, Go, Node.js) and the plan uses third-party images (Signal-CLI, Redis, PostgreSQL operators). Without scanning, known CVEs in base images or dependencies could be deployed to production.
findings.recommendation: Add a CI/CD task to scan all container images with Trivy, Grype, or Snyk before deployment. Block deployment of images with critical/high CVEs. Use minimal base images (distroless, scratch, alpine) where possible. Task 2001 mentions distroless which is good — enforce this pattern across all services.

findings.title: No dependency auditing or version pinning strategy
findings.description: While individual tasks specify dependency versions in Cargo.toml and package.json, there is no systematic task for dependency auditing (cargo audit, npm audit, go vulncheck), no lock file verification, and no policy for addressing vulnerable dependencies.
findings.recommendation: Add a recurring CI task running cargo audit, npm audit/snyk, and govulncheck for each service. Pin all dependency versions (which is default for Cargo.lock and package-lock.json, but verify go.sum is committed). Set up Dependabot or Renovate for automated dependency update PRs.

findings.title: Database connections may not use TLS
findings.description: PostgreSQL connection strings in the ConfigMap reference internal cluster DNS without explicit TLS configuration. While sqlx in Task 2001 includes 'tls-rustls' feature, the connection URL doesn't specify sslmode=require. Redis connections also lack TLS configuration. In-cluster traffic is often unencrypted, which is risky if network policies are misconfigured or if an attacker gains pod-level access.
findings.recommendation: Configure PostgreSQL to require TLS connections (CloudNative-PG supports this via the Cluster CR). Set sslmode=require or sslmode=verify-full in all connection strings. Enable TLS on Redis (Valkey operator supports TLS configuration). This provides defense-in-depth even if network policies have gaps.

findings.title: Morgan agent MCP tools lack authorization scoping
findings.description: MCP tools in Tasks 7004-7006 give the Morgan agent broad access to all backend services (create invoices, run vetting, publish social media, access finance reports) without any authorization scoping. A compromised web chat session or malicious Signal message could potentially trigger admin-level operations through the agent.
findings.recommendation: Implement tool-level authorization in the Morgan agent: classify tools by required privilege level, validate the requesting user's role/permissions before invoking privileged tools (finance reports, social publishing, vetting), and implement per-channel authorization (web chat users should have fewer privileges than authenticated admin users on Signal).

findings.title: File upload validation is minimal
findings.description: Task 6002 validates file types and size for social media uploads (JPEG, PNG, WebP; 10MB limit), but there is no mention of content scanning (malware detection), image re-encoding to strip EXIF/metadata (which can contain GPS data and PII), or protection against zip bombs or decompression attacks.
findings.recommendation: Re-encode uploaded images server-side using a library like sharp (Node.js) or image (Rust) to strip EXIF metadata and normalize the format. Implement virus/malware scanning for uploaded files using ClamAV or a cloud scanning service. Validate image dimensions in addition to file size.

findings.title: No explicit CSRF protection for state-changing endpoints
findings.description: The REST APIs across all services accept POST/PUT/DELETE requests but no task mentions CSRF protection. While service-to-service calls using JWT bearer tokens are inherently CSRF-resistant, browser-based requests from the Next.js frontend to backend APIs could be vulnerable if cookie-based sessions are used (per dp-4 option 2).
findings.recommendation: If session-based auth is chosen (dp-4 option 2), implement CSRF tokens for all state-changing endpoints. If JWT bearer tokens are used, ensure they are sent in the Authorization header (not cookies) to prevent CSRF. Document the CSRF strategy as part of the auth architecture decision.

findings.title: No security event alerting beyond infrastructure metrics
findings.description: Task 9005 and 17 define SLO-based alerting (availability, latency, error rates) and Task 10003-10004 enable audit logging, but there are no alerts specifically for security events: brute force attempts, unusual API key usage patterns, privilege escalation attempts, or anomalous data access patterns.
findings.recommendation: Add security-specific alert rules: alert on >10 failed auth attempts from same source in 5 minutes, alert on Secret access by unexpected service accounts, alert on API calls from unusual source IPs, alert on bulk data access patterns (e.g., listing all customers). Integrate with the audit log pipeline created in 10003/10004.

findings.title: External API credential secrets use placeholder values
findings.description: Task 1006 creates secrets with placeholder values for all external API credentials. While noted as temporary, there is no gate or validation step that prevents deploying to production with placeholder credentials, which could cause silent failures or expose that the system is in a testing state.
findings.recommendation: Add a pre-production validation step that checks all external API secrets have non-placeholder values. Implement a startup check in each service that validates required credentials are non-empty and non-placeholder (e.g., reject values matching 'PLACEHOLDER' or 'changeme').

findings.title: Rate limiter uses fail-open pattern
findings.description: Task 2006 explicitly implements fail-open behavior: if Redis is unavailable, requests are allowed through without rate limiting. While this improves availability, it creates a security gap where an attacker could potentially bypass rate limiting by disrupting the Redis connection.
findings.recommendation: Implement a circuit breaker pattern for the rate limiter: fail-open for a short period (e.g., 30 seconds) with aggressive alerting, then fail-closed if Redis remains unavailable beyond the threshold. Log all fail-open events as security-relevant for monitoring.

findings.title: Vetting results contain aggregated third-party data with unclear retention
findings.description: Task 5001 stores raw OpenCorporates, LinkedIn, Google Reviews, and credit data as JSONB fields. This data may have licensing restrictions on storage duration and redistribution. There is no data retention or purge policy defined.
findings.recommendation: Review third-party API terms of service for data storage restrictions. Implement a data retention policy for vetting results (e.g., purge raw API responses after 90 days, retain only the computed score). Add a background job for data cleanup.

findings.title: Web chat endpoint lacks authentication and abuse prevention
findings.description: Task 7008 implements a WebSocket endpoint for the public website chat widget with only 'basic rate limiting per session.' There is no authentication, CAPTCHA, or bot detection. The session ID is stored in localStorage and can be trivially manipulated. This endpoint is directly exposed to the internet and could be abused for spam, prompt injection attacks against the AI agent, or resource exhaustion.
findings.recommendation: Implement anonymous session tokens with server-side validation (not just localStorage). Add progressive rate limiting that tightens with abuse signals. Implement a CAPTCHA challenge after N messages from an unauthenticated session. Add input sanitization and prompt injection detection before passing user messages to the AI agent. Set connection and message size limits on the WebSocket.

strengths: Task 2001 specifies distroless/scratch runtime images for the Rust services, reducing attack surface significantly
Task 10001-10005 comprehensively addresses Kubernetes RBAC, secret rotation with External Secrets Operator, and multi-layer audit logging
Task 9004 implements a proper default-deny NetworkPolicy approach with explicit allow rules and a documented communication matrix
Task 4004 correctly implements Stripe webhook signature verification with idempotency keys
Task 2006 includes rate limiting with tenant isolation and proper response headers (X-RateLimit-*)
The infrastructure plan uses CloudNative-PG which provides built-in TLS support and automated failover
Tasks 9001-9006 provide comprehensive production hardening including HA, PDB, CDN, and failover testing
Structured error responses are consistently planned across services, reducing information leakage risk
summary: The plan has strong infrastructure security foundations (NetworkPolicies, RBAC, audit logging, secret rotation) but has a critical gap in application-level authentication and authorization — no task implements an auth middleware or token issuance service despite all APIs requiring protection. Database credentials are embedded in a ConfigMap rather than referenced exclusively through Secrets. The public-facing web chat endpoint and Morgan agent MCP tools lack sufficient access controls, creating risk for abuse and unauthorized operations. Adding a dedicated auth service task, fixing credential handling, and hardening the public attack surface would significantly reduce the overall risk profile.
